facts: On March 17, 1917, Loretta Perfectus Walsh became the first woman to officially enlist in the U.S. military in a non-nurse capacity, joining the Navy as a Chief Yeoman.
Ruth Flora became the first woman to enlist as a Navy Hospital Corpsman in July 1948, following the passage of the Women's Armed Services Integration Act.
The Hospital Corps is the most decorated rating in the U.S. Navy. Its members have earned 22 Medals of Honor, 199 Navy Crosses, and 984 Silver Stars.
Rear Admiral Grace Hopper was a pioneer of computer programming. She invented the first compiler and popularized the term 'debugging' after finding a literal moth in her computer.
In 2014, Admiral Michelle J. Howard became the first woman to reach the rank of four-star admiral in the U.S. Navy and the first woman to serve as Vice Chief of Naval Operations.
The title 'Devil Doc' is earned by Hospital Corpsmen serving with the Marines. It is a play on the Marine nickname 'Devil Dog,' signifying the Corpsman has earned the Marines' total trust.
The first twenty women to serve in the Navy Nurse Corps were known as the 'Sacred Twenty.' They were appointed in 1908 and served at naval hospitals in Washington, D.C.
The USS Constitution, launched in 1797, is the oldest commissioned warship afloat in the world. It earned the nickname 'Old Ironsides' during the War of 1812.
In 2010, the Navy changed its policy to allow women to serve on submarines. The first group of female officers reported for duty in late 2011.
Hospital Corpsman Second Class John Bradley was one of the men famously depicted in the Joe Rosenthal photograph raising the U.S. flag on Mount Suribachi during the Battle of Iwo Jima.
Barbara Allen Rainey became the first female Naval Aviator in 1974. She was the first woman to earn her 'Wings of Gold.'
The Naval Act of 1794 authorized the construction of the first six frigates of the U.S. Navy, marking the birth of the permanent American naval force.
In 1993, Lt. Cmdr. Kara Hultgreen became the first female carrier-based fighter pilot in the Navy, flying the F-14 Tomcat.
During the Battle of Chosin Reservoir, Navy Corpsmen were vital to the survival of the 'Chosin Few,' treating thousands of frostbite and combat casualties in sub-zero temperatures.
During WWII, the Navy established the WAVES (Women Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service). By 1945, over 86,000 women had served in the program.
In 2020, Lt. J.G. Madeline Swegle became the Navy's first Black female tactical jet pilot.
Hospital Corpsman Third Class DeWayne T. Williams was posthumously awarded the Medal of Honor for heroics in Vietnam, where he used his body to shield fellow Marines from a grenade.
In 2017, Rear Admiral Nora Tyson became the first woman to lead a U.S. Navy ship fleet (the Third Fleet).
The U.S. Navy was established on October 13, 1775, by the Continental Congress, which authorized the purchase of two armed vessels.
In 1959, Anna Der-Vartanian became the first woman in the U.S. Armed Forces to be promoted to Master Chief Petty Officer (E-9).
The Fleet Marine Force (FMF) Enlisted Warfare Specialist Device was established in 2000 to recognize sailors who have reached the highest level of qualification in Marine Corps combat operations.
Lt. Cmdr. Darlene Iskra was the first woman to command a U.S. Navy ship, the USS Opportune, in 1990.
Before being called 'Hospital Corpsmen' in 1948, medical sailors were known as 'Pharmacist's Mates' or 'Loblolly Boys.'
Rear Admiral Margaret G. Kibben became the first female Chief of Chaplains of the Navy in 2014.
Submarines are known as the 'Silent Service' because they operate undetected and their mission details are often classified.
In 2017, the first woman entered the Navy SEAL officer training pipeline, marking a historic shift in Special Warfare roles.
Mildred McAfee was the first director of the WAVES and the first woman to be commissioned as an officer in the U.S. Naval Reserve.
The U.S. Navy is larger than the next 13 largest navies in the world combined in terms of total fleet tonnage.
In 2021, a female sailor became the first woman to graduate from the Special Warfare Combatant-craft Crewman (SWCC) training.
This 1942 battle was the first in history where opposing carrier fleets never saw or fired directly at each other; all damage was done by aircraft.
In 2022, Lt. Amanda Lee was selected as the first woman to serve as a demonstration pilot for the Blue Angels flight team.
The Hospital Corps was formally established by an act of Congress on June 17, 1898, during the Spanish-American War.
In 2003, Commander Holly Graf became the first woman to command a Navy destroyer, the USS Winston S. Churchill.
In 1975, Donna Tobias became the Navy's first female deep-sea diver, defying the expectations of the era.
Tactical Combat Casualty Care (TCCC) protocols, now used worldwide, were developed by Navy medical personnel to improve survival rates on the battlefield.
Alene Duerk became the first woman in the Navy to reach the rank of Rear Admiral in 1972.
These famous words were spoken by Captain James Lawrence in 1813. They are now the official motto of the U.S. Navy.
In 1978, the first woman graduated from the Navy's Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) school.
During the Vietnam War, 638 Hospital Corpsmen were killed in action and over 4,500 were wounded.
In 1948, Jane Kendeigh was the first flight nurse to land on Iwo Jima and Okinawa to evacuate wounded personnel.
The USS Alfred was the first flagship of the Continental Navy, commissioned in 1775.
Captain Jane G. Dalton was a pioneer in naval medicine, serving as a flight surgeon during a time when women were rare in the field.
Corpsmen serving with Marines wear the Marine Corps uniform with Navy insignia, a tradition that signifies their deep integration with the ground combat element.
In 2021, Captain Amy Bauernschmidt became the first woman to command a U.S. nuclear-powered aircraft carrier, the USS Abraham Lincoln.
The USNS Mercy (T-AH-19) is a massive floating hospital that can be deployed globally to provide trauma care for U.S. forces and humanitarian aid.
In 1991, Commander Rosemary Mariner became one of the first women to serve as the Executive Officer of a naval squadron.
The song 'Anchors Aweigh' was composed in 1906 for the Navy-Army football game and has been the unofficial anthem of the Navy ever since.
In 1917, Loretta Walsh was also the first woman to serve as a recruiter for the United States Navy.
The Navy utilizes 'Fleet Hospitals' (now EMFs) which are modular, rapidly-deployable medical units capable of performing advanced surgery in the field.
In 1961, the Navy appointed its first female Chief Warrant Officer, marking another step in the expansion of female leadership.
The Corpsman's Creed emphasizes the commitment to 'sanctity of life' and 'duty to the wounded,' values that define the rating.
In 2005, Sergeant Leigh Ann Hester (Army) was the first woman to receive the Silver Star for direct combat action, though Navy female Corpsmen have consistently served in high-risk zones.
'Semper Fortis' (Always Courageous) is the unofficial motto of the U.S. Navy, reflecting the spirit of its sailors.
In 1980, Elizabeth Anne Rowe was among the first group of 55 women to graduate from the U.S. Naval Academy at Annapolis.
In the Marine Corps, the title 'Doc' is considered a term of extreme endearment and respect, often placing the Corpsman on a higher social tier than officers in the eyes of the grunts.
In 1985, Rear Admiral Roberta Hazard became the first woman to command a naval training center (NTC San Diego).
This 1942 battle is considered the turning point of the war in the Pacific, where the Navy destroyed four Japanese aircraft carriers.
Commander Elizabeth Belzer was the first woman to serve in the Engineering Duty Officer community in the 1970s.
The Hospital Corpsman Memorial at Arlington National Cemetery honors all the medical personnel who have fallen while serving the Navy and Marine Corps.
In 2012, Vice Admiral Nanette DeRenzi became the first woman to serve as the Judge Advocate General (JAG) of the Navy.
The USS Red Rover, a converted river steamer, was the Navy's first official hospital ship, serving during the Civil War.
Captain Kathryn D. Sullivan was a Navy officer and the first American woman to walk in space in 1984.
The Navy played a critical role in early space exploration; most early astronauts, including Alan Shepard and Neil Armstrong, were Navy pilots.
In 1993, Master Chief Petty Officer Sue Dauser became the first woman to be promoted to Master Diver.
While the Army has Combat Medics, the Navy has Hospital Corpsmen who serve the medical needs of both the Navy and the Marine Corps.
Admiral Lisa Franchetti became the first woman to command a U.S. Navy fleet (the 6th Fleet) in 2018.
'We dive at five' is a common Navy Diver joke, but their official motto is 'He Who Can, Does. He Who Can't, Circulates.'
In 2008, Captain Sara Joyner became the first woman to lead a carrier air wing (CVW-3).
During the Second Battle of Fallujah, Corpsmen were on the front lines, often performing life-saving surgery in bombed-out buildings while under fire.
The first group of female supply officers was integrated into the submarine community in 2011.
The USS Langley (CV-1), commissioned in 1922, was the U.S. Navy's first aircraft carrier.
Vice Admiral Ann Rondeau was a trailblazer in Navy leadership, serving as the Chief of Naval Personnel in the 2000s.
In the 18th century, Navy surgeons were required to provide their own medical instruments and medicines, a far cry from today's advanced equipment.
In 2015, the first female sailor was selected to be a Crew Chief for the Blue Angels.
Admiral Hyman G. Rickover is known as the 'Father of the Nuclear Navy' for his role in developing nuclear-powered submarines and carriers.
Rear Admiral Mary Hall became the first woman to lead the Naval Oceanography program.
Navy medical researchers were instrumental in developing techniques for blood plasma storage during WWII, saving countless lives.
In the late 1970s, the first women began serving as flight instructors, training the next generation of Navy pilots.
The official name for the unit known as SEAL Team 6 is the United States Naval Special Warfare Development Group (DEVGRU).
In 2021, the Navy celebrated the first woman to complete the grueling 37-week training to become a Special Warfare Combatant-craft Crewman.
Modern Navy aircraft carriers have the capability to store and process huge quantities of blood, serving as floating blood banks for the fleet.
In 2012, April Beldo became the first woman to serve as a Fleet Master Chief (for the Office of Naval Manpower).
'The only easy day was yesterday' is the official motto of the Navy SEALs.
In the early 2000s, female pilots began graduating from the elite United States Navy Strike Fighter Tactics Instructor program, better known as TOPGUN.
The HM rating badge features a caduceus, a staff entwined with two serpents, which is an ancient symbol of medicine.
In 1994, Captain Mary McElroy became the first woman to command a major U.S. Naval Station (Guantanamo Bay).
The USS Nautilus (SSN-571), commissioned in 1954, was the world's first nuclear-powered submarine.
In 2022, Lt. Cmdr. Amber Cowan became the first woman to serve as the Executive Officer (XO) of a Navy submarine.
During the Battle of Tarawa, Navy Corpsmen worked under intense fire to treat wounded Marines on the beachhead, often using their own bodies as cover.
In 1979, Rosemary Mariner became the first woman to command a naval aviation squadron.
The phrase 'Sailing the Seven Seas' refers to the Arctic, Antarctic, North Pacific, South Pacific, North Atlantic, South Atlantic, and Indian Oceans.
In the 1990s, women began serving as Surface Warfare Officers (SWO) on combatant vessels, including aircraft carriers.
The first Hospital Corps school was established at Portsmouth, Virginia, in 1902.
In the 1970s, the first women were commissioned into the Navy's Intelligence community.
The Navy's 'Dixie Cup' hat dates back to the late 19th century and was designed to be easily stowed and cleaned.
In 2023, Admiral Lisa Franchetti became the first woman to serve as the Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) and a member of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.
Independent Duty Hospital Corpsmen (IDCs) are highly trained practitioners who operate without a doctor on ships, submarines, and in remote locations.
In 1974, Judith Ann Neuffer became the first woman to be designated as a Naval Flight Officer.
This traditional Navy blessing is used to wish a sailor a safe and prosperous journey.
In the 1980s, the first women began serving as Command Master Chiefs, the most senior enlisted leaders at a command.
Navy Corpsmen have a long tradition of serving as the primary medical responders for the President of the United States and the White House.
In 2023, the Navy announced that the first woman would soon take command of a nuclear-powered submarine.
The official Navy flag was not adopted until 1959. Before that, the U.S. flag was used for all official naval ceremonies.
In 2018, Rear Admiral Mary Riggs was the first woman to serve as the Fleet Surgeon for the U.S. Fleet Forces Command.
This 1944 engagement is considered the largest naval battle in history, involving hundreds of ships and thousands of aircraft.
In 1998, Lt. J.G. Kendra Williams became the first female Navy pilot to fly a combat mission (Operation Desert Fox).
The Hospital Corps seal features a eagle with wings spread, symbolizing the Corps' readiness to deploy anywhere in the world.
In 2020, Captain T.R. Buchanan became the first woman to serve as the Commandant of Midshipmen at the U.S. Naval Academy.
While 'Honor, Courage, Commitment' are the core values, 'Semper Fortis' remains a powerful reminder of naval strength.
In 2024, the Navy welcomed its first female MCPON candidate, reflecting the continued rise of female enlisted leadership.
During WWI, Navy Corpsmen at Belleau Wood were lauded by Marines for their 'utter disregard for their own safety' while treating casualties.
In 2022, the Blue Angels named Lt. Amanda Lee as its first female F/A-18 pilot for the demonstration squadron.
The 'Turtle' was the world's first submarine used in combat, built during the American Revolutionary War.
In the 1970s, women first broke into the command ranks of various Naval Districts across the United States.
The MOD is responsible for the overall medical operations of a facility during their watch, a role Corpsmen often support in leadership roles.
In the 1990s, the first female sailor was named the Navy's Recruiter of the Year, a testament to female impact on force growth.
The Boatswain's Pipe is one of the oldest and most distinctive pieces of equipment used at sea to signal commands to the crew.
Rear Admiral Linda J. Bird was a pioneer in the Supply Corps, reaching the rank of Rear Admiral in the early 2000s.
Navy Corpsmen served in the legendary 'Frozen Chosin' campaign, performing surgery in caves and tents during one of history's coldest battles.
In the 2010s, the first woman served as the Command Master Chief of a Nimitz-class aircraft carrier.
A Navy sea bag contains a sailor's entire life while deployed; the traditional canvas bag is designed to be waterproof and durable.
Lieutenant Kendra Williams made history in 1998 during Operation Desert Fox.
Corpsmen can specialize in dozens of 'C' Schools, from Respiratory Therapy to Deep Sea Diving Medical Technician.
Rear Admiral Katherine L. Gregory became the first woman to lead the Navy's Civil Engineer Corps and the Seabees.
This centuries-old Navy tradition involves a ceremony for sailors who cross the Equator for the first time, turning 'Pollywogs' into 'Shellbacks.'
Rear Admiral Janet R. Donovan reached the rank of Rear Admiral in the JAG Corps in 2012.
The song 'Steadfast and Ready' honors the tradition and sacrifice of the Hospital Corps.
In the late 1980s, the first women graduated from the U.S. Naval Test Pilot School.
The 'First Navy Jack' features 13 stripes and a rattlesnake with the motto 'Don't Tread on Me.'
In the 1980s, the first female Navy officers were assigned as professors at the U.S. Naval Academy.
Navy Corpsmen at Okinawa treated over 50,000 casualties during the 82-day battle, often working 48-hour shifts without sleep.
In 2011, Force Master Chief Sheryl J. Pellerin became the first woman to serve as the Force Master Chief of the Navy's Education and Training Command.
A golden anchor on a ship signifies that the crew has excelled in retention and personnel readiness.
A female officer candidate entered the SEAL training program for the first time in 2017.
Navy Corpsmen are masters of the MEDEVAC, using helicopters and fixed-wing aircraft to transport wounded from the point of injury to surgical care.
In the 1990s, female Captains began commanding major Naval Hospitals and clinics worldwide.
The Sailors' Creed was developed in 1993 to unify the Navy's core values and identity.
In 2013, the Pacific Fleet welcomed its first female Fleet Master Chief, overseeing the largest naval command.
During the Vietnam War, Corpsmen served on the 'Brown Water Navy' patrol boats, providing care in the dense jungle river networks.
In 2010, the first female doctor was assigned to the Blue Angels demonstration team.
'Eternal Father, Strong to Save' is known as the Navy Hymn and is traditionally sung at the end of religious services at sea.
In the early 2000s, female Navy officers first began serving as Naval Attachés in U.S. embassies around the world.
Navy medical researchers have led the world in developing treatments for tropical diseases like malaria to protect forces deployed globally.
Rear Admiral Dawn Cutler became the first woman to lead the Navy's public affairs and media operations in 2014.
This ribbon is earned by sailors who complete 12 months of cumulative sea duty, a badge of pride for the 'Ocean-going' Navy.
Retired Navy Captain Sunita Williams was the first woman to command the International Space Station.
A Corpsman's aid bag (Unit One) is a carefully organized toolkit of life-saving equipment that has evolved from basic bandages to advanced clotting agents.
Admiral Nora Tyson made history by leading the George H.W. Bush Carrier Strike Group in combat operations.
The Navy Diver rating is one of the most physically demanding in the fleet, with a high dropout rate in training.
Since 1898, the Hospital Corps has provided the frontline of defense for the Navy and Marine Corps, ensuring that every sailor and Marine has a 'Doc' at their side.
